title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Install development helper packages R: Install package dependencies (randomForestSRC >= 3.4.0 required):
code: install.packages(c(   "devtools",    # load, test, check, document in one place   "testthat",    # testing framework   "roxygen2",    # builds man/ pages from inline comments   "lintr",       # style linter   "covr",        # test coverage measurement   "pkgdown"      # builds the website )) install.packages(c("randomForestSRC", "randomForest", "ggplot2",                    "dplyr", "tidyr", "survival"))

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Open ggRandomForests.Rproj RStudio/Positron. load package development mode — makes functions available without installing: Confirm works:
code: # Fork the repo on GitHub first, then: git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/ggRandomForests.git cd ggRandomForests  # Add the upstream remote so you can pull future changes git remote add upstream https://github.com/ehrlinger/ggRandomForests.git devtools::load_all()   # shortcut: Ctrl+Shift+L library(randomForestSRC) rf <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris) plot(gg_error(rf))

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Key rule: man/ NAMESPACE files always auto-generated. Run devtools::document() Roxygen change commit updated files.
code: R/                   All source code — one file per function family   gg_*.R             Data extraction functions (return gg_* objects)   plot.gg_*.R        S3 plot methods (return ggplot objects)   help.R             Package-level ?ggRandomForests documentation   zzz.R              .onAttach startup message  man/                 Auto-generated — never edit by hand   *.Rd               Built from Roxygen comments by devtools::document()  tests/   testthat/     test_*.R         One test file per R/ source file  vignettes/   ggRandomForests.qmd  Main package vignette (Quarto)  DESCRIPTION          Package metadata, dependencies, version NAMESPACE            Exports/imports — auto-generated by roxygen2 NEWS.md              Changelog — add an entry for every user-visible change

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: gg_* functions support randomForestSRC randomForest objects. pattern : methods return identically structured gg_* object plot.gg_vimp works either.
code: # 1. Generic — dispatches based on class of `object` gg_vimp <- function(object, ...) {   UseMethod("gg_vimp", object) }  # 2. rfsrc method gg_vimp.rfsrc <- function(object, ...) { ... }  # 3. randomForest method gg_vimp.randomForest <- function(object, ...) { ... }

what: Example: adding a new gg_* function
title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Suppose want add gg_depth() plot average tree depth. skeleton: create R/plot.gg_depth.R following pattern plot.gg_error.R.
code: # R/gg_depth.R  #' Tree depth data object #' #' Extracts average depth statistics per tree from a random forest. #' #' @param object A fitted \code{\link[randomForestSRC]{rfsrc}} or #'   \code{\link[randomForest]{randomForest}} object. #' @param ... Optional arguments passed to methods. #' #' @return A \code{gg_depth} \code{data.frame} with columns \code{ntree} #'   and \code{depth}. #' #' @seealso \code{\link{plot.gg_depth}} #' #' @examples #' rf <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris) #' plot(gg_depth(rf)) #' #' @export gg_depth <- function(object, ...) {   UseMethod("gg_depth", object) }  #' @export gg_depth.rfsrc <- function(object, ...) {   # ... extract depth data ...   gg_dta <- data.frame(ntree = seq_len(object$ntree), depth = depths)   class(gg_dta) <- c("gg_depth", class(gg_dta))   invisible(gg_dta) }

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Always work branch — never commit directly main: development cycle :
code: git checkout -b my-feature-name devtools::load_all()       # reload after editing source devtools::test()           # run tests devtools::document()       # rebuild man/ from Roxygen comments devtools::check()          # full R CMD check (slow — run before PR)

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Tests live tests/testthat/ named test_<source_file>.R match file cover. framework testthat.

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
code: # tests/testthat/test_gg_depth.R test_that("gg_depth returns correct class for rfsrc", {   rf <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50)    gg_dta <- gg_depth(rf)    expect_s3_class(gg_dta, "gg_depth")   expect_s3_class(gg_dta, "data.frame")   expect_true(all(c("ntree", "depth") %in% names(gg_dta)))   expect_equal(nrow(gg_dta), rf$ntree) })  test_that("plot.gg_depth returns a ggplot", {   rf <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50)    gg_plt <- plot(gg_depth(rf))    expect_s3_class(gg_plt, "ggplot") })  test_that("gg_depth throws on wrong input", {   expect_error(gg_depth("not a forest")) })

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Keep forests small tests — ntree = 50 plenty, faster default 1000. Test error path well happy path (expect_error, expect_warning). Use expect_s3_class() rather older expect_is(). Avoid set.seed() unless explicitly testing something random — randomForestSRC results stochastic exact-value tests break across versions. Run tests single file development: Check coverage (aim > 80%):
code: testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test_gg_depth.R") covr::package_coverage()

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: Random forests (Breiman 2001) non-parametric ensemble method requires distributional functional assumptions covariates relate response. method builds large collection de-correlated decision trees via bootstrap aggregation (bagging) random feature selection, averages predictions produce stable, low-variance estimator. randomForestSRC package (Ishwaran Kogalur 2024) provides unified implementation survival, regression, classification forests. ggRandomForests extracts tidy data objects rfsrc fits renders ggplot2 (Wickham 2016), making straightforward explore forest constructed, variables matter, response depends individual predictors. vignette demonstrates complete random forest regression workflow Boston Housing data set (Harrison Rubinfeld 1978; Belsley et al. 1980): Data exploration — EDA scatter panels, variable descriptions Growing forest — fitting RF, checking OOB error convergence Variable selection — VIMP minimal depth via max.subtree() Dependence plots — variable dependence partial dependence via gg_variable() gg_partial_rfsrc() Variable interactions — conditioning plots interactive 3-D partial dependence surfaces plotly
code: library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(randomForestSRC)  if (requireNamespace("ggRandomForests", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggRandomForests) } else if (requireNamespace("pkgload", quietly = TRUE)) {   pkgload::load_all(export_all = FALSE, helpers = FALSE, attach_testthat = FALSE) } else {   stop("Install ggRandomForests (or pkgload for dev builds) to render this vignette.") }  theme_set(theme_bw())

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: Boston Housing data (Harrison Rubinfeld 1978; Belsley et al. 1980) standard benchmark regression. contains data 506 census tracts Boston 1970 census. objective predict median value owner-occupied homes (medv, $1000s) 13 predictors covering crime, zoning, industry, environmental quality, housing characteristics. use copy MASS package (Venables Ripley 2002). plot predictor response (medv), coloring sole categorical variable (chas, whether tract borders Charles River). loess smooth highlights marginal trend.  EDA: predictor vs. median home value, colored Charles River indicator. Even simple view, strong relationship medv lstat (lower status %) rm (rooms per dwelling) apparent. expect random forest confirm important predictors.
code: data(Boston, package = "MASS") Boston$chas <- as.logical(Boston$chas) # nolint: object_name_linter st_labs <- c(   crim    = "Crime rate by town",   zn      = "Residential land zoned > 25k sq ft (%)",   indus   = "Non-retail business acres (%)",   chas    = "Borders Charles River",   nox     = "Nitrogen oxides (10 ppm)",   rm      = "Rooms per dwelling",   age     = "Units built before 1940 (%)",   dis     = "Distance to employment centers",   rad     = "Highway accessibility index",   tax     = "Property tax rate per $10,000",   ptratio = "Pupil-teacher ratio",   black   = "Proportion of Black residents",   lstat   = "Lower status population (%)",   medv    = "Median home value ($1000s)" ) dta <- Boston |>   pivot_longer(c(-medv, -chas), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")  ggplot(dta, aes(x = medv, y = value, color = chas)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +   geom_smooth(aes(x = medv, y = value), color = "grey30",               inherit.aes = FALSE, se = FALSE) +   labs(y = "", x = st_labs["medv"]) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3)

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: grow regression forest using 13 predictors. rfsrc() function detects regression family continuous response. forest grew 500 trees, splitting 5 randomly selected candidate variables node, stopping minimum terminal node size 5.  OOB mean squared error vs. number trees. error stabilizes well 500 trees, indicating forest large enough reliable predictions.  OOB predicted median home values. Points jittered; boxplot shows distribution. point single tract’s OOB prediction. distribution sanity check — interested behind predictions.
code: rfsrc_Boston <- rfsrc(medv ~ ., data = Boston, # nolint: object_name_linter                       importance = TRUE, err.block = 5) rfsrc_Boston #>                          Sample size: 506 #>                      Number of trees: 500 #>            Forest terminal node size: 5 #>        Average no. of terminal nodes: 67.006 #> No. of variables tried at each split: 5 #>               Total no. of variables: 13 #>        Resampling used to grow trees: swor #>     Resample size used to grow trees: 320 #>                             Analysis: RF-R #>                               Family: regr #>                       Splitting rule: mse *random* #>        Number of random split points: 10 #>                      (OOB) R squared: 0.86674919 #>    (OOB) Requested performance error: 11.27124983 gg_e <- gg_error(rfsrc_Boston) gg_e <- gg_e |> filter(!is.na(error)) class(gg_e) <- c("gg_error", class(gg_e)) plot(gg_e) plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_Boston), alpha = 0.5) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(5, 49))

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: VIMP measures increase prediction error variable randomly permuted (Breiman 2001). Large positive values mean variable essential; negative values suggest noise informative.  VIMP ranking. Longer blue bars indicate important variables. lstat rm dominate, clear gap remaining predictors. VIMP values positive, indicating every predictor contributes least marginally. Minimal depth (Ishwaran et al. 2010) ranks variables close root node first split, average. Variables partition large portions population early considered important. threshold 3.01, selecting 6 variables: crim, nox, rm, dis, ptratio, lstat. VIMP minimal depth agree dominance lstat rm. use minimal depth top variables remainder analysis.

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: Variable dependence shows tract’s OOB predicted medv plotted predictor, loess smooth indicating trend.  Variable dependence top predictors (minimal depth rank order). panels confirm EDA suggested: medv decreases sharply lstat increases rm, strongly non-linear ways. remaining variables show weaker still discernible trends.  Variable dependence Charles River (categorical). tracts border Charles River, predicted value distributions largely overlap — consistent chas ranking last VIMP minimal depth. Partial dependence integrates effects covariates, giving risk-adjusted view predictor’s marginal effect (Friedman 2001): f̃(x)=1n∑=1nf̂(x,xi,o) \tilde{f}(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n \hat{f}(x, x_{,o}) use gg_partial_rfsrc(), calls randomForestSRC::partial.rfsrc() directly returns gg_partial_rfsrc object. quickest path plot plot(pd), handles continuous categorical variables automatically. custom layout can also access pd$continuous directly.  Partial dependence top predictors. publication-ready layout custom axis labels, access underlying data frame directly:  Partial dependence (custom styling). lstat shows strongly concave relationship, rm flat region ~6 rooms increasing sharply. non-linear shapes difficult capture simple parametric transforms naturally handled random forest.

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: simplest coplot conditions categorical variable. examine medv vs. lstat, split Charles River status:  Variable dependence lstat, conditional Charles River. decreasing trend holds groups, slightly higher values along Charles River every lstat level. investigate lstat–rm interaction, bin rm six quantile groups using quantile_pts() facet:  Predicted medv vs. lstat, conditional rooms-per-dwelling groups. Median values decrease lstat within every rm group, intercept shifts upward rooms. Smaller homes low-lstat (high-status) neighborhoods still command high prices. complement view — medv vs. rm, conditional lstat groups — completes picture:  Predicted medv vs. rooms, conditional lower-status groups. rm effect strongest low-lstat tracts (bottom-left panels) nearly flat high-lstat tracts, confirming meaningful interaction.
code: gg_v$chas_label <- ifelse(gg_v$chas, "Borders Charles River",                           "Does not border")  plot(gg_v, xvar = "lstat", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = st_labs["medv"], x = st_labs["lstat"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   facet_wrap(~chas_label) rm_pts <- quantile_pts(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$rm, groups = 6, intervals = TRUE) gg_v$rm_grp <- cut(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$rm, breaks = rm_pts) levels(gg_v$rm_grp) <- paste("rm in", levels(gg_v$rm_grp))  plot(gg_v, xvar = "lstat", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = st_labs["medv"], x = st_labs["lstat"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3") +   facet_wrap(~rm_grp) lstat_pts <- quantile_pts(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$lstat, groups = 6,                           intervals = TRUE) gg_v$lstat_grp <- cut(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$lstat, breaks = lstat_pts) levels(gg_v$lstat_grp) <- paste("lstat in", levels(gg_v$lstat_grp))  plot(gg_v, xvar = "rm", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = st_labs["medv"], x = st_labs["rm"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3") +   facet_wrap(~lstat_grp)

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: visualize joint partial dependence medv lstat rm, compute partial dependence grid: 25 values rm, evaluated 25 points along lstat. Interactive partial dependence surface: median home value function lstat rm. surface confirms strong interaction: home values highest lstat low rm high (upper-left corner), dropping steeply along axes. non-planar curvature — particularly sharp step near rm = 7 — demonstrates kind complex, non-linear structure random forests capture naturally.
code: rm_grid <- quantile_pts(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$rm, groups = 25)  surface_list <- lapply(rm_grid, function(rm_val) {   newx <- rfsrc_Boston$xvar   newx$rm <- rm_val   pd_rm <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_Boston, xvar.names = "lstat", newx = newx)   df <- pd_rm$continuous   df$rm <- rm_val   df })  surface_df <- bind_rows(surface_list) if (requireNamespace("plotly", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(plotly)    surface_wide <- surface_df |>     select(lstat = x, rm, medv = yhat) |>     arrange(rm, lstat)    lstat_vals <- sort(unique(surface_wide$lstat))   rm_vals    <- sort(unique(surface_wide$rm))   z_matrix   <- matrix(surface_wide$medv,                         nrow = length(rm_vals),                         ncol = length(lstat_vals),                         byrow = TRUE)    plot_ly(x = lstat_vals, y = rm_vals, z = z_matrix) |>     add_surface(colorscale = "Viridis", showscale = TRUE) |>     layout(       scene = list(         xaxis = list(title = "Lower Status (%)"),         yaxis = list(title = "Rooms per Dwelling"),         zaxis = list(title = "Median Value ($1000s)")       )     ) } else {   message("Install the plotly package for interactive 3D surfaces.")   ggplot(surface_df, aes(x = x, y = rm, fill = yhat)) +     geom_tile() +     scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Median Value") +     labs(x = "Lower Status (%)", y = "Rooms per Dwelling") +     theme_bw() }

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests

logical — partial.rfsrc() fails with logical # predictors in survival forests (randomForestSRC <= 3.5.1). # Exclude the response columns (status, years) from conversion. resp_cols <- c("status", "years") for (nm in setdiff(names(pbc), resp_cols)) {   v <- pbc[[nm]]   if (is.numeric(v) && !is.factor(v) && length(unique(v[!is.na(v)])) <= 5) {     pbc[[nm]] <- factor(v)   } } # Human-readable labels for plot axes st_labs <- c(   status       = "Death Event",   treatment    = "Treatment",   age          = "Age (years)",   sex          = "Female",   ascites      = "Ascites",   hepato       = (gm/dl)",   copper       = "Urine Copper (ug/day)",   alk.phos     = "Alkaline Phosphatase (U/liter)",   ast          = "SGOT (U/ml)",   trig         = "Triglycerides (mg/dl)",   platelet     = "Platelets (per cubic ml/1000)",   prothrombin  = "Prothrombin Time (sec)",   stage        = "Histologic Stage",   years        = "Follow-up Time (years)" ) cls <- sapply(pbc, class) cnt_idx <- pivot_longer(-years, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value",                  values_transform = list(value = as.character)) )  ggplot(dta_cat, aes(x = years, fill = value)) +   geom_histogram(color = "black", binwidth = 1) +   labs(y = "", x = st_labs["years"]) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdBu", na.value = "white") +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y", nrow = 2) +   theme(legend.position = "none") cnt_idx <- union(cnt_idx, which(names(pbc) == "status")) dta_cont <- pbc[, cnt_idx] |>   pivot_longer(c(-years, -status),                names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")  ggplot(dta_cont |> filter(!is.na(value)),        aes(x = years, y = value, color = factor(status), shape = factor(status))) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +   geom_rug(data = dta_cont |> filter(is.na(value)), color = "grey50") +   labs(y = "", x = st_labs["years"], color = "Death", shape = "Death") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks) +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +   theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.2)) pbc_trial <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(treatment)) pbc_test  <- pbc |> filter(is.na(treatment))  gg_km <- gg_survival(interval = "years", censor = "status",                      by = "treatment", data = pbc_trial,                      conf.int = 0.95)  plot(gg_km) +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)",        color = "Treatment", fill = "Treatment") +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.2)) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1.01)) plot(gg_km, type = "cum_haz") +   labs(y = "Cumulative Hazard", x = "Time (years)",        color = "Treatment", fill = "Treatment") +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.8)) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.02, 1.22)) pbc_bili <- pbc_trial |>   mutate(bili_grp = cut(bili, breaks = c(0, 0.8, 1.3, 3.4, 29)))  plot(gg_survival(interval = "years", censor = "status",                  by = "bili_grp", data = pbc_bili), title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: Several predictors PBC trial data contain missing values (cholesterol, copper, triglycerides, others). handle two-step approach: first impute using impute.rfsrc(), uses random forest proximity structure fill missing values, fit survival forest complete imputed data. keeps fitted forest object free imputation state, required partial.rfsrc() work correctly. forest grew 500 trees, splitting 5 randomly selected candidate variables node, stopping minimum terminal node size 15.  OOB prediction error vs. number trees. error stabilizes well 1000 trees, indicating forest large enough reliable predictions.  OOB predicted survival curves. Blue = censored, red = death events. curve represents one patient’s OOB prediction, extended last follow-time. Red (death event) curves generally fall faster, confirming forest discriminates risk groups.  Median predicted survival treatment group 95% confidence bands. non-trial patients (pbc_test) substantial missing data. predict.rfsrc() handles transparently prediction time via na.action = "na.impute" — distinct training-time imputation affect fitted forest object.  Predicted survival non-trial patients (test set). training curves use OOB estimates, plots represent --sample predictions directly comparable.
code: # Step 1: impute missing values via random forest proximity pbc_imputed <- impute.rfsrc(Surv(years, status) ~ .,                              data    = pbc_trial,                              ntree   = 500,                              nimpute = 2)  # Step 2: grow the survival forest on the complete imputed data rfsrc_pbc <- rfsrc(Surv(years, status) ~ .,                    data      = pbc_imputed,                    nsplit    = 10,                    tree.err  = TRUE,                    importance = TRUE) plot(gg_error(rfsrc_pbc)) gg_rsf <- plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc), alpha = 0.2) +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) gg_rsf plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, by = "treatment")) +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.2)) +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) rfsrc_pbc_test <- predict(rfsrc_pbc, newdata = pbc_test,                           na.action = "na.impute",                           importance = TRUE)  plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc_test), alpha = 0.2) +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01))

code: gg_v <- gg_variable(rfsrc_pbc, time = c(1, 3),                     time.labels = c("1 Year", "3 Years"))  plot(gg_v, xvar = "bili", alpha = 0.4) +   labs(y = "Survival", x = st_labs["bili"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) plot(gg_v, xvar = xvar[-1], panel = TRUE, alpha = 0.4) +   labs(y = "Survival") +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.05, 1.05)) plot(gg_v, xvar = xvar_cat, alpha = 0.4) +   labs(y = "Survival") +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.02)) # partial.rfsrc() requires times that match the model's time.interest grid; # gg_partial_rfsrc() snaps the requested values to the nearest observed times. ti   <- rfsrc_pbc$time.interest t1yr <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 1))] t3yr <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 3))]  pd <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, xvar.names = xvar,                        partial.time = c(t1yr, t3yr))  # Quick S3 plot — survival forests produce time-series curves per predictor value plot(pd) ggplot(pd$continuous, aes(x = x, y = yhat,                           color = factor(round(time, 2)),                           group = factor(time))) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   facet_wrap(~name, scales = "free_x") +   labs(y = "Predicted Survival", x = "", color = "Year") +   scale_color_manual(values = setNames(c("steelblue", "firebrick"),                                        as.character(round(c(t1yr, t3yr), 2)))) +   theme_bw() gg_v1 <- gg_variable(rfsrc_pbc, time = 1) gg_v1$edema <- paste("edema =", gg_v1$edema)  plot(gg_v1, xvar = "bili", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = "Survival at 1 Year", x = st_labs["bili"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   facet_grid(~edema) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) albumin_cts <- quantile_pts(gg_v1$albumin, groups = 6, intervals = TRUE) gg_v1$albumin_grp <- cut(gg_v1$albumin, breaks = albumin_cts) levels(gg_v1$albumin_grp) <- paste("albumin =",                                     levels(gg_v1$albumin_grp))  plot(gg_v1, xvar = "bili", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = "Survival at 1 Year", x = st_labs["bili"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   facet_wrap(~albumin_grp) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01))

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: richer view interaction bilirubin albumin, construct partial dependence surface. compute partial dependence grid 25 albumin values, evaluated 25 bilirubin points. Interactive partial dependence surface: survival function bilirubin albumin. surface shows survival highest bilirubin low albumin high (upper-left corner), drops steeply bilirubin increases albumin decreases. non-planar shape surface — particularly steep gradient low albumin high bilirubin — confirms interaction detected conditional plots. Brier score measures prediction accuracy point event-time grid comparing predicted survival probabilities observed outcomes, inverse-probability--censoring weighting (IPCW) account right-censoring (Graf et al. 1999). gg_brier() wraps randomForestSRC::get.brier.survival() returns tidy data frame ready plotting.  Time-resolved Brier score PBC survival forest. score starts near zero (subjects alive, predictions trivially correct), peaks around median event time uncertainty greatest, decreases remaining -risk pool shrinks. 15–85% per-subject envelope shows spread individual Brier contributions time. narrow ribbon indicates forest assigns similar predicted risks across subjects; wide ribbon points heterogeneous risk predictions.  Brier score 15–85% per-subject envelope. running CRPS (continuous ranked probability score) integrates Brier score time, normalised elapsed time. provides single time-averaged accuracy measure penalises poorly calibrated predictions throughout follow-period.  Running CRPS PBC survival forest. integrated CRPS — scalar summary overall calibration — stored attribute can retrieved :
code: # Create grid of albumin values alb_grid <- quantile_pts(pbc_trial$albumin, groups = 25)  # For each albumin value, compute partial dependence on bili at ~1 year surface_list <- lapply(alb_grid, function(alb_val) {   newx <- pbc_trial[, rfsrc_pbc$xvar.names, drop = FALSE]   newx$albumin <- alb_val   pd_alb <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, xvar.names = "bili",                               newx = newx, partial.time = t1yr)   df <- pd_alb$continuous   df$albumin <- alb_val   df })  surface_df <- bind_rows(surface_list) if (!exists("surface_df")) {   message("surface_df not available — skipping plotly surface (see surface-data chunk error above).") } else if (requireNamespace("plotly", quietly = TRUE)) {   # Reshape for surface   library(plotly)    surface_wide <- surface_df |>     select(bili = x, albumin, survival = yhat) |>     arrange(albumin, bili)    # Create matrix form   bili_vals <- sort(unique(surface_wide$bili))   alb_vals  <- sort(unique(surface_wide$albumin))   z_matrix  <- matrix(surface_wide$survival,                        nrow = length(alb_vals),                        ncol = length(bili_vals),                        byrow = TRUE)    plot_ly(x = bili_vals, y = alb_vals, z = z_matrix) |>     add_surface(colorscale = "Viridis", showscale = TRUE) |>     layout(       scene = list(         xaxis = list(title = "Bilirubin"),         yaxis = list(title = "Albumin"),         zaxis = list(title = "Survival")       )     ) } else {   message("Install the plotly package for interactive 3D surfaces.")   # Fallback: contour plot with ggplot2   ggplot(surface_df, aes(x = x, y = albumin, fill = yhat)) +     geom_tile() +     scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Survival") +     labs(x = "Bilirubin", y = "Albumin") +     theme_bw() } gg_bs <- gg_brier(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_bs) plot(gg_bs, envelope = TRUE) plot(gg_bs, type = "crps") attr(gg_bs, "crps_integrated") #> [1] 1.389769

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: vignette demonstrated complete random survival forest analysis using randomForestSRC ggRandomForests: Kaplan–Meier curves via gg_survival() confirmed treatment effect PBC trial, consistent Fleming Harrington (1991). OOB error via gg_error() showed forest stabilized quickly. VIMP via gg_vimp() minimal depth via max.subtree() identified bilirubin, albumin, copper, prothrombin, age key predictors — matching proportional hazards model. Variable dependence via gg_variable() revealed non-proportional hazards effects bilirubin copper. Partial dependence via gg_partial_rfsrc() provided risk-adjusted confirmation supported log-transforms used parametric models. Conditional plots interactive surfaces exposed bilirubin–albumin interaction. Brier score CRPS via gg_brier() quantified time-resolved prediction accuracy overall calibration survival forest. ggRandomForests design separates data extraction plotting: every gg_*() function returns tidy data frame can plotted package’s plot() methods fed directly custom ggplot2 workflows.

------------- pbc data # Load a cached randomForestSRC object # We need to create this dataset data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC",) #> Warning: data set ‘’ not found # For whatever reason, the age variable is in days... makes no sense to me for (ind in seq_len(dim(pbc)[2])) {  if (!is.factor(pbc[, ind])) {    if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {      if (sum(range(pbc[, ind], na.rm = TRUE) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }    }  } else {    if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {      if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }      if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(FALSE, TRUE)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }    }  }  if (!is.logical(pbc[, ind]) &      length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 5) {    pbc[, ind] <- factor(pbc[, ind])  } } #Convert age to years pbc$age <- pbc$age / 364.24  pbc$years <- pbc$days / 364.24 pbc <- pbc[, -which(colnames(pbc) == "days")] pbc$treatment <- as.numeric(pbc$treatment) pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 1)] <- "DPCA" pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 2)] <- "placebo" pbc$treatment <- factor(pbc$treatment) dta_train <- pbc[-which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ] # Create a test set from the remaining patients pbc_test <- pbc[which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ]  #======== # build the forest: rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(years, status) ~ .,  dta_train,  nsplit = 10,  na.action = "na.impute",  tree.err = TRUE,  forest = TRUE,  importance = TRUE,  save.memory = TRUE )   gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta) #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Outcome"

text: rf_model fitted rfsrc object. xvar.names Character vector predictor names partial dependence computed. Must subset rf_model$xvar.names. xvar2.name Optional single character name grouping variable newx. supplied, partial dependence computed separately unique level variable grp column appended. newx Optional data.frame predictor values evaluate partial effects . Defaults training data stored rf_model$xvar. column names must match rf_model$xvar.names. partial.time Numeric vector desired time points survival forests (ignored regression/classification).  Values automatically snapped nearest entry rf_model$time.interest — see Survival forests section .  NULL (default), three quartile points time.interest used. partial.type Character; type predicted value survival forests, passed partial.rfsrc. One "surv" (default), "chf", "mort". Ignored non-survival forests. partial.rfsrc() requires non-NULL value survival families; supplying avoids cryptic “argument length zero” error underlying C code. cat_limit Variables fewer cat_limit unique values newx treated categorical; others continuous. Defaults 10. n_eval Number evaluation points continuous variables. Instead passing observed values (can slow, especially survival forests), continuous predictors evaluated quantile grid many points. Categorical variables always use unique levels. Defaults 25.

code: ## Construct mock varpro partialpro output: ##   - "age": a continuous predictor (xvirtual has > 10 points) ##   - "sex": a categorical predictor (xvirtual has 2 points) set.seed(42) n_obs <- 30   # number of observations (rows in yhat matrices) n_pts <- 15   # number of evaluation points for continuous variables  mock_data <- list(   age = list(     # xvirtual: evaluation grid for the marginal effect curve     xvirtual   = seq(30, 80, length.out = n_pts),     # xorg: original observed values (used only for categorical detection)     xorg       = sample(seq(30, 80, by = 5), n_obs, replace = TRUE),     # yhat matrices: n_obs rows x n_pts columns (predictions at each grid pt)     yhat.par   = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.nonpar = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.causal = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs)   ),   sex = list(     # Two categories: the xvirtual grid has only 2 points     xvirtual   = c(0, 1),     xorg       = sample(c(0, 1), n_obs, replace = TRUE),     # Two-column yhat matrices (one column per category)     yhat.par   = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.nonpar = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.causal = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs)   ) )  result <- gg_partialpro(mock_data)  ## Continuous result: one row per evaluation grid point head(result$continuous) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   variable parametric nonparametric  causal name  #>      <dbl>      <dbl>         <dbl>   <dbl> <chr> #> 1     30      -0.0695       -0.120   0.436  age   #> 2     33.6     0.149        -0.477   0.0438 age   #> 3     37.1    -0.237        -0.270   0.0531 age   #> 4     40.7    -0.0346       -0.0673 -0.0482 age   #> 5     44.3    -0.136         0.171   0.176  age   #> 6     47.9     0.0217        0.0159  0.119  age    ## Categorical result: n_obs rows per category level head(result$categorical) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   parametric nonparametric causal variable name  #>        <dbl>         <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl> <chr> #> 1      0.850       -0.0338  0.751        0 sex   #> 2      1.76        -0.901  -0.829        0 sex   #> 3      0.846       -1.18    0.710        0 sex   #> 4     -0.545       -0.448   0.950        0 sex   #> 5      0.255        0.559  -0.579        0 sex   #> 6      0.299        0.0931 -1.23         0 sex

-------- pbc data # We need to create this dataset data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC", ) #> Warning: data set ‘’ not found # For whatever reason, the age variable is in days... # makes no sense to me for (ind in seq_len(dim(pbc)[2])) {   if (!is.factor(pbc[, ind])) {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(range(pbc[, ind], na.rm = TRUE) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   } else {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(FALSE, TRUE)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   }   if (!is.logical(pbc[, ind]) &     length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 5) {     pbc[, ind] <- factor(pbc[, ind])   } } # Convert age to years pbc$age <- pbc$age / 364.24  pbc$years <- pbc$days / 364.24 pbc <- pbc[, -which(colnames(pbc) == "days")] pbc$treatment <- as.numeric(pbc$treatment) pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 1)] <- "DPCA" pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 2)] <- "placebo" pbc$treatment <- factor(pbc$treatment) dta_train <- pbc[-which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ] # Create a test set from the remaining patients pbc_test <- pbc[which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ]  # ======== # build the forest: rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(years, status) ~ .,   dta_train,   nsplit = 10,   na.action = "na.impute",   forest = TRUE,   importance = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE )  gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta)   # Restrict to only the top 10. gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_pbc, nvar = 10) plot(gg_dta)

code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Regression forest -- one continuous curve per variable ## ------------------------------------------------------------ set.seed(42) airq <- na.omit(airquality) rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., data = airq, ntree = 50)  pd <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_airq, xvar.names = c("Wind", "Temp"),                        n_eval = 10) plot(pd)   # \donttest{ ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Survival forest -- one curve per requested time horizon, ## faceted by variable. Y-axis label tracks `partial.type`. ## ------------------------------------------------------------ # randomForestSRC's formula parser requires the unqualified Surv() symbol; # it Depends on `survival`, so Surv is on the search path once # randomForestSRC is loaded. data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") set.seed(42) rfsrc_v <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ .,                                   data = veteran, ntree = 50) ti  <- rfsrc_v$time.interest t30 <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 30))] t90 <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 90))]  # Default partial.type = "surv" -> y-axis "Predicted Survival" pd_s <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_v, xvar.names = "age",                          partial.time = c(t30, t90), n_eval = 8) plot(pd_s)   # partial.type = "chf" -> y-axis "Predicted CHF" pd_c <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_v, xvar.names = "age",                          partial.time = c(t30, t90),                          partial.type = "chf", n_eval = 8) plot(pd_c)  # }

code: ## ggRandomForests does not depend on the varpro package; we construct a ## minimal mock of the partialpro() output so the example runs everywhere. set.seed(42) n_obs <- 30 n_pts <- 15 mock_data <- list(   age = list(     xvirtual    = seq(30, 80, length.out = n_pts),     xorg        = sample(seq(30, 80, by = 5), n_obs, replace = TRUE),     yhat.par    = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.nonpar = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.causal = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs)   ),   sex = list(     xvirtual    = c(0, 1),     xorg        = sample(c(0, 1), n_obs, replace = TRUE),     yhat.par    = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.nonpar = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.causal = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs)   ) )  pp <- gg_partialpro(mock_data) result <- plot(pp)  # Continuous predictors get one panel per variable; categorical get # box-plots over the parametric / nonparametric / causal effect types. result$continuous  result$categorical   # Restrict to one or two effect types plot(pp, type = "nonparametric") #> $continuous  #>  #> $categorical  #>  plot(pp, type = c("parametric", "causal")) #> $continuous  #>  #> $categorical  #>

code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- iris data # Build a small classification forest (ntree=50 keeps example fast) set.seed(42) rfsrc_iris <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_iris)  plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Regression example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- air quality data # na.action = "na.impute" handles missing Ozone / Solar.R values set.seed(42) rfsrc_airq <- rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., data = airquality,                     na.action = "na.impute", ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_airq)  plot(gg_dta)   ## -------- mtcars data set.seed(42) rfsrc_mtcars <- rfsrc(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_mtcars)  plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Survival example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- veteran data ## randomized trial of two treatment regimens for lung cancer data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") set.seed(42) rfsrc_veteran <- rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., data = veteran, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran) plot(gg_dta)   # With 95% pointwise bootstrap confidence bands gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran, conf.int = .95) plot(gg_dta)   # Stratified by treatment arm gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran, by = "trt") plot(gg_dta)   ## -------- pbc data (larger dataset -- skipped on CRAN) # \donttest{ data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC") # For whatever reason, the age variable is in days; convert to years for (ind in seq_len(dim(pbc)[2])) {   if (!is.factor(pbc[, ind])) {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(range(pbc[, ind], na.rm = TRUE) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   } else {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(FALSE, TRUE)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   }   if (!is.logical(pbc[, ind]) &     length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 5) {     pbc[, ind] <- factor(pbc[, ind])   } } # Convert age from days to years pbc$age <- pbc$age / 364.24 pbc$years <- pbc$days / 364.24 pbc <- pbc[, -which(colnames(pbc) == "days")] pbc$treatment <- as.numeric(pbc$treatment) pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 1)] <- "DPCA" pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 2)] <- "placebo" pbc$treatment <- factor(pbc$treatment) # Remove test-set patients (those with no assigned treatment) dta_train <- pbc[-which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ]  set.seed(42) rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(years, status) ~ .,   dta_train,   nsplit = 10,   na.action = "na.impute",   forest = TRUE,   importance = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE )  gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta)   gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, conf.int = .95) plot(gg_dta)   gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, by = "treatment") plot(gg_dta)  # }

Panel plot across all predictors plot(gg_dta,   xvar = rfsrc_iris$xvar.names,   panel = TRUE, se = FALSE ) #> Warning: Ignoring unknown parameters: `se`   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## regression ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- air quality data # na.action = "na.impute" handles missing Ozone / Solar.R values set.seed(42) rfsrc_airq <- rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., data = airquality,                     na.action = "na.impute", ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_airq)  # Treat Month as an ordinal factor for better visualisation gg_dta[, "Month"] <- factor(gg_dta[, "Month"])  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Wind") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Temp") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Solar.R") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' #> Warning: Removed 7 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range (`stat_smooth()`). #> Warning: Removed 7 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).   # Panel plot across continuous predictors plot(gg_dta, xvar = c("Solar.R", "Wind", "Temp", "Day"), panel = TRUE) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' #> Warning: Removed 7 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range (`stat_smooth()`). #> Warning: Removed 7 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). #> Warning: Removed 7 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).   # Factor variable uses notched boxplots plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Month", notch = TRUE) #> Warning: Ignoring unknown parameters: `notch` #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`?   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## survival examples ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- veteran data data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") set.seed(42) rfsrc_veteran <- rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., veteran,   nsplit = 10,   ntree = 50 )  # Marginal survival at 90 days gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_veteran, time = 90)  # Single-variable dependence plots plot(gg_dta, xvar = "age") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "diagtime") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'   # Panel coplot for two predictors at a single time plot(gg_dta, xvar = c("age", "diagtime"), panel = TRUE) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'   # Compare survival at 30, 90, and 365 days simultaneously gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_veteran, time = c(30, 90, 365))  # Single-variable plot (one facet per time point) plot(gg_dta, xvar = "age") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'   # Panel coplot across two predictors and three time points plot(gg_dta, xvar = c("age", "diagtime"), panel = TRUE) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

text: Computes partial dependence curves survival competing-risk rfsrc forest calling partial.rfsrc npts evenly-spaced unique values predictor across stored event times.

title: Survival partial dependence data for one or more predictors — surv_partial.rfsrc
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## survival ## ------------------------------------------------------------  data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") v.obj <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time,status)~.,   veteran, nsplit = 10, ntree = 100)  spart <- surv_partial.rfsrc(v.obj, var_list="age", partial.type = "mort") #> Warning: 'surv_partial.rfsrc' is deprecated and will be removed in a future release. #> Use 'gg_partial_rfsrc()' instead, which returns a classed object with a #> dedicated plot() method. #> partial plot for: age  ## partial effect of age on mortality partial.obj <- partial(v.obj,                        partial.type = "mort",                        partial.xvar = "age",                        partial.values = v.obj$xvar$age,                        partial.time = v.obj$time.interest) pdta <- get.partial.plot.data(partial.obj)  plot(lowess(pdta$x, pdta$yhat, f = 1/3),      type = "l", xlab = "age", ylab = "adjusted mortality")   ## example where x is discrete - partial effect of age on mortality ## we use the granule=TRUE option partial.obj <- partial(v.obj,                        partial.type = "mort",                        partial.xvar = "trt",                        partial.values = v.obj$xvar$trt,                        partial.time = v.obj$time.interest) pdta <- get.partial.plot.data(partial.obj, granule = TRUE) boxplot(pdta$yhat ~ pdta$x, xlab = "treatment", ylab = "partial effect")    ## partial effects of karnofsky score on survival karno <- quantile(v.obj$xvar$karno) partial.obj <- partial(v.obj,                        partial.type = "surv",                        partial.xvar = "karno",                        partial.values = karno,                        partial.time = v.obj$time.interest) pdta <- get.partial.plot.data(partial.obj)  matplot(pdta$partial.time, t(pdta$yhat), type = "l", lty = 1,         xlab = "time", ylab = "karnofsky adjusted survival") legend("topright", legend = paste0("karnofsky = ", karno), fill = 1:5)    ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## competing risk ## ------------------------------------------------------------  data(follic, package = "randomForestSRC") follic.obj <- rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., follic, nsplit = 3, ntree = 100)  ## partial effect of age on years lost partial.obj <- partial(follic.obj,                        partial.type = "years.lost",                        partial.xvar = "age",                        partial.values = follic.obj$xvar$age,                        partial.time = follic.obj$time.interest) pdta1 <- get.partial.plot.data(partial.obj, target = 1) pdta2 <- get.partial.plot.data(partial.obj, target = 2)  # Save and restore the user's graphical parameters per CRAN policy. oldpar <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) on.exit(par(oldpar)) par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(lowess(pdta1$x, pdta1$yhat),      type = "l", xlab = "age", ylab = "adjusted years lost relapse") plot(lowess(pdta2$x, pdta2$yhat),      type = "l", xlab = "age", ylab = "adjusted years lost death")  ## partial effect of age on cif partial.obj <- partial(follic.obj,                        partial.type = "cif",                        partial.xvar = "age",                        partial.values = quantile(follic.obj$xvar$age),                        partial.time = follic.obj$time.interest) pdta1 <- get.partial.plot.data(partial.obj, target = 1) pdta2 <- get.partial.plot.data(partial.obj, target = 2)  matplot(pdta1$partial.time, t(pdta1$yhat), type = "l", lty = 1,         xlab = "time", ylab = "age adjusted cif for relapse") matplot(pdta2$partial.time, t(pdta2$yhat), type = "l", lty = 1,         xlab = "time", ylab = "age adjusted cif for death")

title: Recover original variable names from varpro one-hot encoded feature names — varpro_feature_names
text: varpro_names character vector names output varpro (may include one-hot encoded suffixed names "sex0", "sex1") dataset original data frame passed varpro, used look valid column names

title: Recover original variable names from varpro one-hot encoded feature names — varpro_feature_names
text: character vector unique original variable names (suffixes)

title: Recover original variable names from varpro one-hot encoded feature names — varpro_feature_names
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Simple case: one continuous variable + one binary factor ## ------------------------------------------------------------------ ds <- data.frame(age = c(25, 30, 45), sex = c("M", "F", "M"))  # varpro one-hot encodes 'sex' into 'sex0' and 'sex1' varpro_names <- c("age", "sex0", "sex1") varpro_feature_names(varpro_names, ds) #> [1] "age" "sex" # Returns: c("age", "sex")  ## ------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Multi-level factor: three-level 'group' variable ## ------------------------------------------------------------------ ds2 <- data.frame(score = 1:6,                   group = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), 2)))  # varpro appends 0/1/2 for each level vn2 <- c("score", "group0", "group1", "group2") varpro_feature_names(vn2, ds2) #> [1] "score" "group" # Returns: c("score", "group")  ## ------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Already-clean names pass through unchanged ## ------------------------------------------------------------------ ds3 <- data.frame(x = 1:5, y = 1:5) varpro_feature_names(c("x", "y"), ds3) #> [1] "x" "y" # Returns: c("x", "y")

title: ggRandomForests v2.7.3
text: autoplot() S3 methods 10 gg_* classes, delegating corresponding plot.gg_*() method objects work |> pipelines, patchwork, cowplot compositions via ggplot2::autoplot(). print() summary() S3 methods every gg_* data object (gg_error, gg_vimp, gg_rfsrc, gg_variable, gg_partial, gg_partial_rfsrc, gg_partialpro, gg_roc, gg_survival, gg_brier). print() header-— use head() rows. summary() returns printable summary.gg object per-class diagnostics. gg_* constructor now attaches "provenance" attribute (source, family, ntree, n, xvar.names) consumed new methods. New gg_brier() extractor plot.gg_brier() method time-resolved Brier scores CRPS survival forests (issue #9). Wraps randomForestSRC::get.brier.survival() adds mortality-quartile decomposition, 15-85 percent per-subject envelope, running CRPS via trapezoidal integration. Supports cens.model = c("km", "rfsrc"), type = c("brier", "crps"), envelope (overall line + 15-85% ribbon). Multi-model comparison left dplyr::bind_rows() multiple gg_brier outputs — see ?gg_brier example. Visual unification ribbon overlays across plot methods. ribbons now use shared alpha (.gg_ribbon_alpha = 0.2) shared fill (.gg_ribbon_fill = "steelblue") single-series cases (KM/NA CIs, bootstrap CIs, gg_brier envelope); group-stratified ribbons keep group-coloured fill. Statistical bounds unchanged — styling. First-class varPro integration restore variable-selection workflow disappeared randomForestSRC::var.select.rfsrc(). title: ggRandomForests v2.7.1
text: Fix gg_partial_rfsrc() survival forests: partial.rfsrc() called without partial.type, causing zero-length comparison ((partial.type == "rel.freq") ...) inside C-level prediction routine aborting call. Survival forests now pass partial.type = "surv" (default; configurable via new partial.type argument accepting "surv", "chf", "mort"). unblocks partial-dep chunk survival vignette. Fix gg_partial_rfsrc() survival forests multiple partial.time values: get.partial.plot.data() returns yhat [length(partial.values) x length(partial.time)] matrix, previous code assumed vector crashed column-mismatch assigning time. result now reshaped long form (x, time) pair single row. Improve plot.gg_partial_rfsrc() survival layout: predictor value now x-axis one curve per (rounded) time point coloured Time, faceted variable name. previous default put time x-axis one curve per predictor value, producing saturated legend dozens nearly-identical lines. Add tests/testthat/test_plot_layer_data.R: regression suite uses ggplot2::layer_data() verify plot.gg_*() method renders non-empty layers every supported forest family. Catches empty-figure class bug (transform/plot column-name mismatch) without requiring visual inspection. ggrandomforests.news() now reads NEWS.md (canonical change log R also surfaces via utils::news()). legacy hand-maintained inst/NEWS removed — silently drifted v2.4.0 (June 2025) across three releases, users running helper saw stale version info. One source truth, drift window. Fix plot.gg_vimp() legend duplication: bar geom mapped fill color positive column, fill legend titled “VIMP > 0”, leaving redundant second legend titled “positive”. aesthetics now share “VIMP > 0” title ggplot merges single legend default. Fix plot.gg_vimp() forests -positive VIMP: bar geom previously mapped color (fill), producing hollow / outline- bars “Ignoring unknown labels: fill” warning whenever labs(fill = ...) applied. fill color now mapped unconditionally, bars render filled every case. Add @examples blocks plot.gg_partial_rfsrc() plot.gg_partialpro(). latter uses self-contained mock varpro::partialpro() output structure example runs without pulling varpro dependency.

title: ggRandomForests v2.7.0
text: S3 design overhaul: gg_partial(), gg_partialpro(), gg_partial_rfsrc() now stamp return values S3 classes (gg_partial, gg_partialpro, gg_partial_rfsrc respectively), enabling plot() dispatch without boilerplate. Add plot.gg_partial(), plot.gg_partial_rfsrc(), plot.gg_partialpro() S3 methods; continuous predictors render line plots, categorical bar charts, faceted variable name. Survival forests produce curves time; two-variable surface plots group xvar2.name. Convert gg_survival() S3 generic dispatching class first argument. New gg_survival.rfsrc() method extracts survival response directly fitted forest (separate data argument needed); gg_survival.default() preserves existing interface. Fix plot.gg_survival() auto-coercion: previously called gg_survival(rfsrc_obj) treating forest interval string argument, causing latent crash; replaced inherits() guard. Deprecate surv_partial.rfsrc() via .Deprecated() pointer gg_partial_rfsrc(); package tests updated suppress warning. Fix gg_partial_rfsrc() — make_eval_grid() used unlist(dplyr::select()) coerced factor columns integer codes; now uses newx[[xname]] preserve column class. Categorical detection extended cover .factor() .character() addition cardinality check. Add guards gg_partial_rfsrc(): -NA xval NA removal now emits warning skips variable; -NA grouping variable (xvar2) calls stop(); n_eval cat_limit validated single integers >= 2 near function entry. Fix cyclomatic complexity across gg_partial_rfsrc.R: refactored eight top-level unexported helpers (validate_scalar_int, validate_partial_args, snap_partial_time, make_eval_grid, call_partial_rfsrc, partial_one_var, partial_no_group, partial_with_group, split_partial_result); functions now score cyclocomp_linter limit 20. Fix @param partial.time documentation: “see section ” corrected “see section ”. Replace deprecated tidyr::gather() tidyr::pivot_longer() plot.gg_vimp() plot.gg_partialpro(). Add gg_survival.rfsrc, gg_survival.default, plot.gg_partial, plot.gg_partial_rfsrc, plot.gg_partialpro NAMESPACE; add corresponding @rdname / @export roxygen tags. Update tests: add expect_s3_class() checks new classes; add plot() smoke tests gg_partial, gg_partial_rfsrc, gg_partialpro; add gg_survival.rfsrc tests KM extraction, stratification, error non-survival forest. Add plot.gg_partial, plot.gg_partial_rfsrc, plot.gg_partialpro _pkgdown.yml reference index.

title: ggRandomForests v2.7.0
text: Fix critical visual bug plot.gg_rfsrc: aes() calls used bare string literals instead .data[[col]], causing every aesthetic map constant string rather underlying data column. plot types (regression, classification, survival) affected. Fix aes() bare-string literals plot.gg_roc multi-class branch; remove unreachable (crv < 2) dead-code branch. Fix bootstrap_survival CI-band indexing gg_rfsrc: negative index computed via colnames() -op large datasets latent crash data ≤ 2 unique event times. Fix gg_rfsrc.rfsrc: .null(df[, col]) detect missing columns; replaced !col %% colnames() guard. Fix gg_rfsrc.randomForest: method used non-existent object$xvar; now recovers training frame via .rf_recover_model_frame(). Fix legend suppression plot.gg_error single-outcome forests data frame variable column. Fix gg_vimp plot.gg_vimp: 1:nvar replaced seq_len(nvar) S3 methods; 1:0 silently returned c(1, 0) instead integer(0) nvar == 0. Migrate full test suite testthat 3.x API: expect_is → expect_s3_class / expect_type / expect_true(.*()); expect_equivalent → expect_equal(ignore_attr = TRUE); context() calls removed; testthat 1.x expect_that / is_identical_to removed. Add .lintr package-level linter configuration; fix lintr spacing gg_partial. Improve GitHub Actions: lint.yaml now fails CI lint issue; R-CMD-check.yaml treats warnings errors uses Rtools 44; test-coverage.yaml duplicate codecov upload removed. Add covr vdiffr Suggests.
